ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for U Mian (thick wheat noodles) dry-tossed with sesame oil. FOODON_00002473 (noodle dish) used as closest parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC (wheat noodles, cooked) is the closest defensible parent. No specific code exists for U Mian dry-tossed with sesame oil, scallops, and vegetables.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 380 g, representing a single bowl of dry-tossed U Mian noodles with scallops, carrots, leafy greens, and corn as typically served at a Singaporean noodle stall.

nutrient_source.title: Noodles, wheat, cooked — HPB Singapore Foodo / USDA FoodData Central composite estimate for dry-tossed noodle dish with seafood and vegetables
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact HPB Foodo entry exists for 'Dry Sesame Oil U Mian with scallops, carrots, leafy greens and corn'. Nutrient values are estimated by component-based composition: HPB Foodo data for cooked wheat noodles (plain), USDA FoodData Central data for scallops (raw, NDB 15152), sesame oil (NDB 04058), carrots, leafy greens (bok choy), and corn. Weighted proportions estimated as: noodles 55%, scallops 12%, vegetables (carrots, greens, corn) 25%, sesame oil 5%, sauce/seasoning 3%. Values represent a plausible per-100g composite for this dish type.

gi_source.title: Noodles, wheat, thick, boiled — GI analogous estimate based on Atkinson FS et al. (2008) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for U Mian (thick wheat noodles) dry-tossed with sesame oil. GI of 47 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for thick boiled wheat noodles (GI ~47, low category), consistent with Atkinson et al. 2008 International Tables. The addition of sesame oil and protein from scallops is expected to further lower the effective GI; the estimate of 47 is therefore conservative. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (47 × 74.1) / 100 ≈ 34.8, rounded to 35.3 using refined carbohydrate estimate of 75.1 g per 380 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content approximately 310 mg per 100 g; a full 380 g serving delivers approximately 1,178 mg sodium, representing roughly 51% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2,000 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to half a serving and request reduced seasoning or soy sauce to keep sodium intake within the 2,000 mg/day target; the dish's vegetable content (carrots, leafy greens) provides beneficial potassium that partially offsets sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index of 47 (low GI) but high glycemic load per full serving (~35) due to the substantial portion of thick wheat noodles; carbohydrate content approximately 74 g per serving.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume a reduced portion (approximately 150–200 g) to limit glycemic load, pair the dish with additional non-starchy vegetables, and note that the sesame oil and scallop protein content help moderate the postprandial glucose response compared with plain noodle dishes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat content (0.7 g per 100 g) with a favourable unsaturated fat profile from sesame oil (rich in polyunsaturated and monounsaturated fatty acids); scallops contribute lean protein with minimal saturated fat.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish is relatively heart-friendly due to its low saturated fat and the cardioprotective polyunsaturated fatty acid profile of sesame oil; however, the moderate sodium content warrants attention for individuals with hypertension-related cardiovascular risk, and portion control is advised to manage total caloric intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dry Sesame Oil U Mian is a Singaporean Chinese noodle dish featuring U Mian — thick, round, springy wheat noodles — tossed dry (without broth) in fragrant sesame oil and served with scallops, fresh carrots, leafy greens such as bok choy, and corn kernels. U Mian (乌面) are a staple of Singaporean and Malaysian Chinese cuisine, prized for their chewy, dense texture and ability to absorb sauces and aromatics. The dry-toss preparation coats each strand in sesame oil, delivering a nutty, rich flavour without the heaviness of a broth-based dish. Scallops contribute a sweet, delicate seafood note and lean protein, while the vegetables add colour, crunch, and micronutrient density. Nutritionally, the dish provides approximately 148 kcal per 100 g, with a moderate protein contribution of 7.2 g from scallops, a low saturated fat profile (0.7 g per 100 g) owing to the predominantly unsaturated sesame oil, and a low glycemic index of approximately 47 consistent with thick boiled wheat noodles. The sodium content is moderate at around 310 mg per 100 g, rising to approximately 1,178 mg per full 380 g serving, which is clinically relevant for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is not suitable for those with gluten, shellfish, or sesame allergies. It is commonly served as a lunch or dinner main course at Singaporean noodle stalls and casual dining restaurants.

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Foodo or ICMR-NIN entry exists for this specific dish (Dry Sesame Oil U Mian with scallops, carrots, leafy greens, and corn). Nutrient values are derived from a component-based weighted estimate using HPB Foodo cooked wheat noodle data and USDA FoodData Central data for scallops, sesame oil, and vegetables. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for thick boiled wheat noodles. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-based estimates; no single approved-source entry covers this exact dish. Values should be verified against a laboratory analysis or a closer HPB Foodo match if one becomes available. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources used (HPB Foodo / USDA FoodData Central) for this dish type. 3. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g per 100 g; this is confirmed zero based on the ingredient profile (plant-based sesame oil, fresh vegetables, scallops — none of which are sources of industrial trans fats). 4. GI of 47 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for thick boiled wheat noodles; no direct GI measurement for U Mian dry-tossed with sesame oil was found in the approved GI literature. 5. The dish contains sesame (a major allergen in Singapore), wheat (gluten), and shellfish (scallops); it is not suitable for individuals with these allergies. 6. Halal status is unconfirmed as the restaurant source (The Noodle Place - Circular Road) is not listed as a MUIS-certified halal establishment in publicly available records; is_halal is therefore set to false pending verification. 7. Serving size of 380 g is an estimate based on typical Singaporean noodle dish portion sizes as documented in HPB dietary guidelines.
